published.title: The Before-and-After Prompt
published.content: Most people write their first prompt like a search query: short, vague, hoping Claude reads their mind. Here's the single most impactful change you can make: add context before your request. Instead of 'Write an email about the deadline,' try: 'I'm a program manager at DC CAP writing to scholarship applicants who haven't submitted their FAFSA yet. Write a warm, encouraging reminder email that explains why the FAFSA matters for their DC CAP scholarship and gives them a clear deadline. Keep it under 200 words.' The difference is dramatic. Context transforms Claude from a generic text generator into a knowledgeable colleague.
published.tryThis: Take a task you did last week. Write the worst possible prompt for it (under 10 words). Then rewrite it with context, audience, format, and constraints. Compare the outputs.

published.title: Delegation: The Decision Before the Prompt
published.content: Delegation is the competency most people skip. Before you type a single word into Claude, you've already made a delegation decision: 'Is this a task AI should help with?' Good delegation means understanding both what Claude does well (drafting, summarizing, analyzing patterns, formatting) and what requires human judgment (final decisions on student welfare, sensitive communications, anything involving live relationships). The Delegation Map activity in Week 3 will help you map your recurring tasks on a grid. For now, start noticing: every time you open Claude, you've made a delegation choice. Was it a good one?
published.tryThis: Keep a running list today of every task you considered using Claude for. At the end of the day, mark each one: 'Good delegation' or 'Should have done this myself.' You'll start seeing patterns.

published.title: Chat vs. Cowork: Pick the Right Tool
published.content: Chat (claude.ai) is your everyday thinking partner. Use it for quick drafts, brainstorming, one-off analysis, and anything where you need text output you'll copy elsewhere. Cowork (the desktop app) is your production studio. Use it when you need actual files — Word docs, PowerPoints, Excel workbooks — or when you want DC CAP's built-in Skills to handle the formatting and voice rules for you. A good rule of thumb: if you're going to copy-paste Claude's output into another application, Chat is fine. If you want Claude to produce the final deliverable directly, use Cowork.
published.tryThis: Open both Chat and Cowork today. Ask each the same question: 'Draft a one-paragraph summary of what DC CAP does for a new donor.' Compare the experience. Notice which felt more natural for this task.

published.title: Anthropic's AI Fluency Index: What 10,000 Conversations Reveal
published.content: In February 2026, Anthropic published the AI Fluency Index — an analysis of 9,830 multi-turn conversations to identify what separates effective AI users from everyone else. The headline finding: iteration is the strongest predictor of fluency. Users who revised their prompts and pushed back on outputs demonstrated 2.67 additional fluency behaviors compared to 1.33 in users who accepted the first response. The uncomfortable finding: users are least critical of polished-looking outputs. Clean formatting and confident language reduce scrutiny — exactly when scrutiny matters most. This is why we built the 'Polished Output Trap' into Activity 4.
published.tryThis: Next time Claude gives you a response that looks great on first read, pause. Ask yourself: 'What would I check if a new hire wrote this?' Then check those things.

published.title: Your Anthropic Academy Certificate Matters
published.content: The AI Fluency for Nonprofits course you're completing this week was built in partnership with GivingTuesday and specifically adapted for organizations like DC CAP — teams with limited resources, multiple stakeholder accountabilities, and mission-driven work. The certificate you earn is recognized by Anthropic and signals that you've completed structured training in the 4D framework. Beyond the credential: the course covers the same competencies we'll practice hands-on during the pilot. Think of it as the conceptual foundation. The pilot activities are where you apply it to DC CAP's actual work.
published.tryThis: When you finish the course, screenshot your certificate and share it in the pilot Slack channel. Seeing your cohort complete the same milestone builds momentum.

published.title: Discernment Starts With One Question
published.content: Claude will almost always give you a confident, well-formatted answer. That confidence is the exact moment your Discernment competency matters most. Discernment, one of the four core competencies in DC CAP's AI Fluency Framework (alongside Delegation, Description, and Diligence), is the ability to critically evaluate AI-generated outputs for quality, accuracy, relevance, and bias. In practice, it begins with a single question: "How would I verify this if a new colleague handed it to me?" Anthropic's own research found that structured prompting techniques like step-by-step reasoning and few-shot examples improved accuracy by 20% in one enterprise case study. That means a well-structured prompt yields better output. But even a well-structured prompt can produce plausible-sounding errors. A scholarship renewal reminder that cites the wrong GPA threshold. A board summary that rounds enrollment numbers in a misleading direction. A donor email that attributes a statistic to a report that doesn't exist. These errors look polished. They read smoothly. And that is precisely why Discernment requires deliberate practice. The DC CAP AI Fluency Framework breaks Discernment into three layers: evaluating output quality, assessing whether the human-AI collaboration is productive, and gathering feedback to refine AI behaviors over time. You are building a verification habit. The federal government has begun formalizing AI literacy as a workforce competency, with the Department of Labor publishing an AI literacy framework in early 2026. DC CAP's 4D framework already embeds this skill. Your job now is to practice it consistently.
published.tryThis: Open your most recent Claude conversation. Pick one factual claim in the output and verify it using an independent source. Notice how long the verification takes versus how long you spent reviewing the original output. That ratio tells you something about your current Discernment habits.
